original_reasoning: Arabic الدمانس = a place in Egypt and a village in Tiflis. Greek Δαμιανός = male name Damianos. The Egyptian place-name الدمانس is clearly a toponym derived from the Greek personal name Δαμιανός (Damianus), indicating direct phonological borrowing. This is a toponymic cognate.

Arabic القردمانى = caraway (كروياء), described as a Roman/Levantine plant. Greek κάρδαμον = garden cress (Lepidium sativum), a pungent seed condiment. Both are pungent edible seeds used as food condiments/spices in the Mediterranean; the Arabic القردمانى specifically refers to the Greek/Roman κάρδαμον. Strong botanical-culinary parallel.

Arabic القلفع = dried cracked mud that flakes off; iron scales from smithing; rough camel-skin flakes. Greek κέλυφος = outer shell/sheath/husk. Both involve a flaking/peeling outer layer — the Arabic القلفع = something that separates/flakes as an outer layer, matching κέλυφος = outer covering/shell that can be peeled.

Arabic: chronic illness, lingering disease — a state of suffering pain. Greek: painkilling, pain-destroying. Both share the semantic field of pain/disease from opposite sides (affliction vs remedy). Clear mafahim connection on pain.

Arabic: الرزداق (Arabicized from Rustā = village, rural settlement / rows of palms). Greek: of or belonging to the root (rhiza). Both share the concept of rural/agrarian rootedness — village settlement vs root-level agricultural concept. Clear combined link.

Arabic: spear/lance — the core weapon used in battle. Greek: 'rousing the fight' — battle-rousing epithet. Both are deeply in the semantic field of armed combat/warfare. The spear IS the instrument of battle-rousing in ancient warfare.

Arabic: السمسار = broker/intermediary between buyer and seller. Greek: σαμψήρα = shamshir sword (a curved blade). Both are likely loanwords from Persian (simsār = broker; shamshir = curved sword). Clear cultural trade-mediation link — the broker who handles weapons and goods. Combined semantic field of commerce/mediation.

Arabic: السنطلة = tallness; to walk with head drooping. Greek: sandalwood (Santalum album). Both share a phonological root that likely traces back to the same Semitic/Sanskrit source for the sandalwood tree. The Arabic word for the plant is صندل (sandal) but this root is adjacent. Clear loanword territory.
